title: Whittled birch hair comb
subtitle: A wide-tooth comb carved from a thin birch blank
excerpt: A wide-tooth hair comb whittled from a thin piece of seasoned birch: 14 cm long, five teeth, and a spine wide enough to grip. The teeth are cut with stop cuts and fine knife strokes; birch's close grain allows teeth as narrow as 4 mm without splitting. A gift-quality piece from a small offcut.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for comb and toothed tool carving; carved wooden combs are documented in Victorian craft and domestic manuals.

content.text: A wooden comb requires a wood with close, interlocked grain that will not split along a tooth under light pressure. Birch is the standard: close-grained, pale, smooth-finishing, and stiff enough to keep the teeth rigid without being so brittle that the thin sections snap. The technique is the same as tine-cutting in a serving fork, scaled down and repeated five times across the tooth section. The
stop cut
at the base of each tooth space is the gate that prevents the knife from splitting the grain into the adjacent tooth.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and stop cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest.
Sharpening: the detail knife must be sharp enough to lift a thin birch curl without pressure. Strop every 15 minutes.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A piece of
seasoned

birch: 16 cm long, 5 cm wide, 7 mm thick. The grain must run along the 16 cm length; cross-grained stock will split between the teeth. Birch is available from craft timber merchants as thin planed stock; alternatively, re-saw a thicker piece on a bandsaw to 7 to 8 mm. The
blank
should be flat and without cup or bow. A warped piece will produce a comb that does not lie flat.
Method

Draw the comb profile: spine 4 cm wide and 5 mm thick (the full width of the blank on the non-tooth side); tooth section 10 cm long and 3.5 mm thick. Five teeth, each 6 mm wide at the base, tapering to 4 mm at the tip, with 5 mm spaces between. Mark the four spaces as parallel lines across the tooth section face.
Score a
stop cut

across the grain at the base of each tooth space, perpendicular to the blank face; pressing the sloyd knife tip in to 2 mm depth. This is the boundary that holds each tooth separate from its neighbours.
With the detail knife, make a
push cut

from the tip of each tooth space toward the stop cut, removing the waste between each pair of teeth in thin slices. Work on the face of the blank; then flip and work the other face. Remove equal amounts from both faces to keep the teeth centred through the thickness.

Refine the tooth taper with light push cuts along the tooth face; the teeth should taper from 6 mm at the base to 4 mm at the rounded tip. Round the tooth tips with the sloyd knife; a flat-ended tooth is sharp and will snag hair.
Shape the spine into a comfortable curve on its back edge: the spine sits in the palm when combing; a rounded spine is more comfortable than a flat one. Round the two spine corners.
Finishing

Sand through 120, 180, and 220 grit, including all tooth faces and the spaces between them. A folded strip of sandpaper reaches the spaces. Apply two thin coats of Danish oil; Danish oil does not build up in the tooth spaces as beeswax or oil-and-wax products can. The comb is ready to use when the oil is fully dry; 24 hours after the second coat.
Variations
Fine-tooth comb: seven teeth at 4 mm each with 3 mm spaces; requires a sharper detail knife and more care in the stop-cut phase.
Cherry or walnut: both fine-grained and suitable. Cherry dries to a warm tone; walnut is darker and heavier.
Chip-carved spine: simple chip-carving patterns on the spine face are consistent with the scale and add decorative detail to a gift piece.
Care
Wipe clean with a dry cloth. Avoid wetting the comb; water will raise the grain and roughen the tooth surfaces over time. Re-oil twice a year.

items.fix: Always score the stop cut before the waste removal cut. If the grain is angled, the detail knife must follow the grain direction on the waste-removal pass, not the blank axis.
items.cause: Stop cut at the tooth base was not established before removing waste between teeth; or the grain runs at a slight angle to the blank axis
items.symptom: Tooth splits from the adjacent tooth along the grain

items.fix: Round each tooth tip with a thumb-pivot cut arced around the tip in two or three passes; not a single push cut straight across the end.
items.cause: Rounding done with a flat push cut rather than a curved stroke
items.symptom: Tooth tips are sharp after rounding
